input.query_title: Random graphs with arbitrary degree distributions and their applications
input.query_abstract: Recent work on the structure of social networks and the internet has focussed attention on graphs with distributions of vertex degree that are significantly different from the Poisson degree distributions that have been widely studied in the past. In this paper we develop in detail the theory of random graphs with arbitrary degree distributions. In addition to simple undirected, unipartite graphs, we examine the properties of directed and bipartite graphs. Among other results, we derive exact expressions for the position of the phase transition at which a giant component first forms, the mean component size, the size of the giant component if there is one, the mean number of vertices a certain distance away from a randomly chosen vertex, and the average vertex-vertex distance within a graph. We apply our theory to some real-world graphs, including the world-wide web and collaboration graphs of scientists and Fortune 1000 company directors. We demonstrate that in some cases random graphs with appropriate distributions of vertex degree predict with surprising accuracy the behavior of the real world, while in others there is a measurable discrepancy between theory and reality, perhaps indicating the presence of additional social structure in the network that is not captured by the random graph.
input.hyp_generic_object: sparse random constraint-satisfaction instances with an arbitrary variable-occurrence and clause-arity distribution
input.hyp_abstract: Random instances of sparse constraint-satisfaction problems undergo abrupt solvability transitions as the ratio of constraints to variables increases, and the location of these thresholds controls the difficulty of the hardest instances for search procedures. Prior analyses largely assumed every variable participates in a Poisson-distributed number of constraints; real instance ensembles and structured encodings instead exhibit heavy-tailed, irregular participation. We give a generating-function analysis of ensembles in which each variable's number of constraint incidences, and each constraint's arity, follow prescribed arbitrary distributions. Representing these distributions by their probability generating functions and modeling the local incidence neighborhood as a tree, exact in the sparse limit, we derive the critical constraint density at which an extensive, mutually-dependent core of clauses first crystallizes, the point beyond which naive unit-propagation-style peeling fails. From derivatives of the generating functions at unit argument we obtain the expected number of variables within a bounded reasoning radius, the mean size of the residual core, and the fraction of variables it engulfs above threshold. The treatment extends to encodings with distinct positive- and negative-incidence spectra. On synthetic ensembles with engineered incidence distributions, predicted peeling thresholds and core sizes match empirical solver behavior closely, whereas deviations on structured instances expose incidence correlations that violate the tree approximation.
input.candidate_abstract: Community definitions usually focus on edges, inside and between the communities. However, the high density of edges within a community determines correlations between nodes going beyond nearest-neighbours, and which are indicated by the presence of motifs. We show how motifs can be used to define general classes of nodes, including communities, by extending the mathematical expression of Newman-Girvan modularity. We construct then a general framework and apply it to some synthetic and real networks.
